text: UNITED KINGDOM CONSTITUTIONAL FRAMEWORK. The UK does not have a single codified constitution. Its constitutional law is found in statutes, conventions, judicial decisions, and authoritative works. KEY PRINCIPLES: (1) PARLIAMENTARY SOVEREIGNTY: Parliament is the supreme legal authority and can make or repeal any law. No body can override or set aside legislation passed by Parliament (A.V. Dicey). This was qualified by EU membership (1973-2020) and is now reasserted post-Brexit. (2) RULE OF LAW: All persons and authorities in the state are bound by and entitled to the benefit of laws publicly made and publicly administered (Dicey; Lord Bingham in R (Bancoult) v SSFCA). (3) SEPARATION OF POWERS: While the UK does not have a strict separation (the executive sits in the legislature), there is an institutional separation between the judiciary, legislature, and executive, reinforced by the Constitutional Reform Act 2005. (4) CONSTITUTIONAL CONVENTIONS: Unwritten rules of political practice that regulate the conduct of government (e.g., the Salisbury Convention, collective cabinet responsibility, the convention that the monarch acts on ministerial advice).

source_name: Magna Carta (1215) and Key UK Constitutional Statutes
text: KEY UK CONSTITUTIONAL STATUTES. MAGNA CARTA (1215): The Great Charter, signed by King John, established the principle that the king is subject to law. Chapter 39 (now 29): 'No free man shall be seized, or imprisoned, or dispossessed, or outlawed, or exiled, or injured in any way, nor will we enter on him or send against him except by the lawful judgment of his peers or by the law of the land.' BILL OF RIGHTS 1689: Established fundamental rights including the prohibition of cruel and unusual punishment, the right to petition the Crown, free elections, and freedom of speech in Parliament. Also established parliamentary sovereignty over the Crown. HUMAN RIGHTS ACT 1998: Incorporated the European Convention on Human Rights into UK domestic law. Courts must interpret legislation compatibly with Convention rights where possible (section 3). Courts can issue declarations of incompatibility (section 4). Public authorities must act compatibly with Convention rights (section 6). CONSTITUTIONAL REFORM ACT 2005: Established the Supreme Court of the United Kingdom, separated the judicial function from the House of Lords, and reformed the office of Lord Chancellor to protect judicial independence. EUROPEAN COMMUNITIES ACT 1972/ EUROPEAN UNION (WITHDRAWAL) ACT 2018: The 1972 Act gave effect to EU law in the UK. The 2018 Act repealed it and converted existing EU law into retained domestic law (Brexit legislation).

UK ADMINISTRATIVE LAW. JUDICIAL REVIEW: The process by which courts supervise the exercise of public power. Grounds for judicial review (established in Council of Civil Service Unions v Minister for the Civil Service [1985] — the GCHQ case): (1) ILLEGALITY — the decision-maker must correctly understand the law that regulates the decision-making power and must give effect to it. (2) IRRATIONALITY (Wednesbury unreasonableness) — the decision must not be so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could have reached it. (3) PROCEDURAL IMPROPRIETY — the decision-maker must observe procedural requirements imposed by statute and the rules of natural justice (the right to be heard and the rule against bias). PROPORTIONALITY: An additional ground increasingly applied in human rights cases — the measure must be proportionate to the legitimate aim pursued. The Supreme Court in R (Keyu) v Secretary of State [2015] and Bank Mellat v HM Treasury [2013] adopted a structured proportionality test.
